results.title: Modeling the Effect of Replacing Sugar-Sweetened Beverage Consumption with Water on Energy Intake, HBI Score, and Obesity Prevalence
results.abstract: <jats:p>Sugar-sweetened beverages (SSB) contribute to excessive weight gain through added energy intake. Replacing SSB with water is one strategy that has shown promise in helping lower excessive energy intake. Using nationally representative data from US adults (n = 19,718) from NHANES 2007–2012 we examine the impact of replacing SSB with water on Healthy Beverage Index (HBI) scores and obesity prevalence. This study aimed to analyze the change in taxed and non-taxed SSB consumption in the Thai population. Method: This cohort study was carried out in 2018 and 2019. results.abstract: <jats:p>Dental caries is a major oral health problem, particularly among developing countries. results.abstract: <jats:p>Background/Objectives: More than half of Swiss adults exceed the World Health Organization’s recommended limit for free sugar intake, with sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) as a major contributor. SSB intake may be associated with other dietary risk factors, but little is known about diet quality excluding SSB intake. Therefore, the objective of this study was to investigate the association of SSB intake with the non-SSB diet quality in Swiss adults. Methods: Data from the cross-sectional,

results.abstract: <jats:p>This study determined predictors of child classification SSBs (flavored milk, fluid and powdered juice drinks, iced tea, energy drinks and soda) consumption by using inductive methods to predict behavior. Study included 462 low‐income caregivers of 3–5 y. old children. Two‐third of children consumed SSBs. results.title: Abstract P217: Neighborhood and Individual Characteristics Associated With Sugar-sweetened Beverage Consumption Among Overweight and Obese Adults in the Portal Obesity Cohort
results.abstract: <jats:p>A diet high in sugar sweetened beverages (SSB), which includes soda and beverages with added sugar, has been shown to be associated with adverse health outcomes. National data indicate that one-half of adults and two-thirds of youth consume at least one SSB per day. Studies have suggested that proximity to SSB vendors and neighborhood food environment are not associated with consumption; however higher intake has been shown in adults with lower individual level education and income. results.title: The impact of taxes and price promotions on sugar-sweetened beverage consumption
results.abstract: <jats:p>Excessive consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages (SSBs) is a risk factor for obesity, cardiovascular diseases, type-2 diabetes, and other chronic conditions. The high SSB consumption may be partly due to the low price relative to other beverages. Policies targeting SSB prices, such as soda taxes, have been increasingly implemented as a public health measure to reduce sugar consumption and/or a mean to increase revenue for other public services.
